I want a `summarize` agent task to start from a COPY of the conversation/session another agent task `explore` ended with, without mutating `explore`. How do I express that on the <Task>, and what are the rules — does it create a dependency, what kind of task can be a fork source, and how does it behave inside a <Loop>?

verify.rubric: Correct answer uses the Task `fork` prop set to the source task id. It must say: the fork source becomes an implicit dependency (this task waits for the source to complete), it copies the source's final agent session snapshot into a fresh independent session and submits its own prompt, the source is never mutated, the fork source must be an AGENT task (fork requires an agent task), and inside a <Loop> it resolves to the latest completed snapshot for that id.

input.task: Build a prompt-extraction quality gate using the seeded <LoopUntilScored>: the caller reads the latest draft score from ctx, passes it as currentScore, requires a threshold of 0.9, allows at most 6 iterations, keeps looping while the score is pending, returns the last draft rather than failing at the cap, and wraps a draft <Task> that has a scorer attached for observability. Include the component props and the child task shape.

verify.rubric: A correct answer must use <LoopUntilScored idPrefix currentScore={latest?.score ?? null} threshold={0.9} maxIterations={6} onMaxReached="return-last" onPending="continue"><Task ... scorers={...}>...</Task></LoopUntilScored>. It should state the component is a thin wrapper around <Loop id={`${idPrefix}:loop`}> and exits when currentScore >= threshold; pending scores continue by default.

Include the real component and the important props.

input.task: I am going to register a recurring nightly maintenance workflow with 'smithers cron add ...'. Author the workflow file itself: a top-level sequence where (1) an agent task scans the repo for stale branches, then (2) a loop iterates over each stale branch and (3) does that branch's cleanup work inside its own isolated worktree. Use real Smithers components so it renders with 'smithers graph'.

With the Smithers Effect API, write a fan-out review that mounts the SAME reusable shard twice without durable-ID collisions. Build a `Smithers.fragment` (`F`) with two steps, `read-diff` and `summarize` (summarize `needs` read-diff). Then in a `Smithers.workflow` handle `G`, run both mounts concurrently with `G.parallel`, mounting the fragment under two scope prefixes `api` and `web` using `G.scope`, and finalize with `G.from`. Explain in a comment what step IDs end up in the database for each mount.

verify.rubric: Correct code must: build the shard with Smithers.fragment(inputSchema) and F.step(...), compose with F.sequence; in the host G.from( G.parallel( G.scope('api', shard), G.scope('web', shard) ) ); and note that G.scope applies 'instanceId.' as a durable ID prefix so the persisted step IDs are api.read-diff / api.summarize and web.read-diff / web.summarize (no collision). It must use G.scope for multi-mount (not just copy-paste the steps), must use G.parallel for concurrency, and must NOT call G.from on the fragment.

input.task: Write a Smithers workflow for a release fan-out that wraps a budgeted implementation sequence in a real error boundary. Use the actual Smithers components; do not invent React ErrorBoundary APIs.

Author a Smithers workflow for hot-mode self-intervention during a gateway incident. It should first gather the failing run context, require a human approval before any file edits, then use the built-in meta-orchestrator to read a markdown strategy and modify only `packages/gateway/**/*.ts`, and finally report what changed. The workflow must be suitable to launch with `smithers up incident.tsx --hot true`; do not invent a `<SelfModify>` component.
